data.title: ESF reserves reach HK$3.75 billion, even as government subsidies shrink
data.description:
The reserves of Hong Kong’s largest international school group have more than tripled to HK$3.75 billion (US$480.8 million) in over a decade, even as it taps different income sources to offset shrinking government subsidies, the South China Morning Post has found by reviewing the organisation’s annual reports.
The English Schools Foundation (ESF) also said in its annual report for the 2024-25 academic year that it expected increased competition for students and it would modernise its marketing strategies to attract the children of talent and investors settling in the city.
The report showed that its net assets, which matched its reserves, reached a record HK$3.75 billion, surpassing Lingnan University’s HK$2.65 billion and the Education University of Hong Kong’s HK$2.04 billion for their 2024-25 financial year ending last June.
The SCMP review of the ESF’s financial reports found that its reserves were more than three times the HK$1.18 billion recorded in 2012-13 and had grown continually for more than 10 years.
The reserves came from the group’s operational surplus, which comprised tuition fee income and capital funds from nomination rights.
In a reply to the SCMP, an ESF spokesman said: “We have to accumulate financial reserves that will allow us to renew, refresh and rebuild our schools when they require it. The overwhelming majority of our reserves are ring-fenced for this purpose.”
He added that the ESF would not receive any financial support from the government for its campus redevelopment, as authorities started phasing out subsidies in 2016-17 and would cease in 2028-29.
In its latest report, the ESF said its schools needed to be rebuilt near the end of their useful lives – a period of about 60 years.
The stockpile comprised six components – a HK$1.45 billion building reserve, a HK$875 million investment fund, a HK$830 million general reserve, a HK$286 million school reserve, a HK$260 million capital fund and a HK$52 million development fund.
The group’s operating income stood at HK$3.24 billion in 2024-25, up by 4.1 per cent from 2023-24, with the increase attributed to higher revenue from tuition fees, which accounted for 86 per cent of operating income.

Government grants accounted for only 3.3 per cent of income, compared with about 17.7 per cent in 2013-14.
In the report, the group also confirmed that it bought a two-storey office in North Point last year to serve as its headquarters. The grade A office reportedly cost HK$440 million.
“[It is] the first time that we have owned our own headquarters in our nearly 60 years of existence. This is more than just a new building from which we can support our schools. It is a statement of intent,” the ESF said in its latest report.
“It says loud and clear that Hong Kong is our home and that we are fully invested in its future.”
The group, set up in 1967, operates 22 schools and kindergartens in Hong Kong, providing English-medium education that follows the International Baccalaureate curriculum.
With government grants shrinking, the ESF has been tapping different sources of income over the years.
It launched a nomination rights scheme in 2012, charging up to HK$500,000 per student for priority for an interview and on the waiting list.
In 2015, the group launched a non-refundable capital levy, a one-off payment for students accepting a placement offer.
Under the levy, each Year One student is required to pay HK$38,000. The sum is reduced on a sliding scale for those who join in later grades.
In its latest report, the ESF said student enrolment continued to be an area of focus for all its schools, but noted there were some risks ahead, both locally and internationally.
“With the uncertain global economy and the increased number of non-local curriculum offerings in Hong Kong, competition for student enrolment is expected to intensify,” the report said.
“To combat this, the group is focusing its efforts on modernising our marketing strategies to attract new audiences across Hong Kong, including [holders of] various talent passes and [those working in] family offices in Hong Kong.”
The report also said its “ESF Explore” initiative – which offers a wide range of extracurricular programmes and summer courses open to children aged six months to 18 years – generated non-tuition income that contributed to the group’s finances.
“Our summer camps have also seen a growing number of families from mainland China, as well as mainland Chinese residents based in Hong Kong joining our programmes,” the ESF said.
The group added that it would find ways to capture this “engaged audience” as part of its overall admissions strategy.

data.title: Moore Threads posts US$4.3m profit as Nvidia rival surges amid Beijing chip push
data.description:
Moore Threads Technology, one of China’s challengers to Nvidia, swung to a profit in the first quarter of 2026, as domestic graphics processing unit (GPU) champions capitalised on surging computing demand amid Beijing’s push for chip self-sufficiency.
The Beijing-based firm reported a net profit of 29.4 million yuan (US$4.3 million) for the January to March period, compared with a net loss of 112.5 million yuan a year earlier, according to a filing to the Shanghai Stock Exchange on Sunday.
Quarterly revenue jumped 155 per cent year on year to 737.6 million yuan.
Moore Threads’ quarterly profit followed a stellar 2025, when its revenue tripled to 1.5 billion yuan and its net loss narrowed by 36.7 per cent to 1.02 billion yuan.
“In 2026, Moore Threads will continue to align with national strategies by focusing on core technology breakthroughs and accelerating the roll-out of next-gen chips,” said founder and CEO Zhang Jianzhong in the 2025 annual report released on the same day. Zhang is a former China head of Nvidia.
At the end of March, the company disclosed a 660 million yuan order for its KUAE computing cluster, which is based on tens of thousands of interconnected GPUs, without revealing the client.
Moore Threads has been making a major push for its flagship MTT S5000 chip and KUAE computing cluster to meet the booming demand for computing power, especially for large-scale artificial intelligence infrastructure.
The company is among a handful of domestic GPU champions seen as potential challengers to Nvidia, as the US giant faces trade restrictions in selling its most advanced AI chips to China.
Beijing-based Cambricon Technologies reported five-fold sales growth for 2025 while achieving its first full-year profit since listing in 2020.
Meanwhile, Shanghai-based MetaX Integrated Circuits reported a doubling of annual revenue while narrowing its loss by nearly half.

data.title: OpenClaw adds DeepSeek V4 models as tech world assesses Huawei tie-up
data.description:
OpenClaw has adopted DeepSeek’s new V4 Flash as the default model for the popular artificial intelligence agent, while the global tech community scrutinises the implications of the Chinese AI firm’s major update optimised to run on Huawei Technologies’ chips.
In an update on Sunday, OpenClaw said it had expanded its catalogue to include DeepSeek’s V4 Flash and its flagship V4 Pro models, and had integrated other capabilities such as Google Meet into the agent. OpenClaw also optimised how DeepSeek V4 models could remain consistent when performing multi-step tasks.
The move came two days after Hangzhou-based DeepSeek released its open-source V4 models, with the V4 Pro boasting 1.6 trillion parameters, making it the company’s biggest-ever model by that metric, while the smaller V4 Flash model has 284 billion parameters.
DeepSeek said on Friday that its V4 models were optimised for mainstream agent tools including Anthropic’s Claude Code, OpenClaw and Tencent Holdings’ CodeBuddy.

The DeepSeek V4 release drew global attention as its collaboration with Huawei, which announced “full support” from its Ascend chips and supernode systems for model inference, put China’s home-grown AI hardware to the test.
China’s state media hailed the Ascend-compatible DeepSeek models as “a signal of advancement in [China’s] software-hardware collaboration”.
“Although China is currently trailing in process nodes and single-card performance, we will explore new solutions under existing constraints by leveraging system design, cluster architecture, hardware-software synergy and power efficiency,” said Yuyuan Tantian, an influential social media account run by state broadcaster CCTV, on Sunday.
Huawei said on Friday that the newest Ascend 950PR and 950DT chips achieved “day zero” adaptation to serve DeepSeek’s latest V4 model for inference. DeepSeek did not disclose the hardware stack used to train V4, but said the development of “kernels” – codes dictating the functions of graphics processing units – was adapted to both Nvidia and Huawei chips in the technical report.
Reuters reported on Friday that Huawei chips were used for part of V4 Flash’s training process as well, which requires more advanced chips than those used for inference. Huawei declined to comment on the claim when reached by the South China Morning Post on Sunday.
Hing Shing Leung, a Hong Kong-based equity analyst, said he specifically “couldn’t find any proof that Ascend was used to train V4 Flash”. However, he noted that the technical document from DeepSeek showed a deepening integration between the two companies.
Huawei’s support for the DeepSeek ecosystem was a “possible indication that Ascend 950 will be used to train its model in future”, Leung said.
Additional reporting by Vincent Chow

data.title: Li Auto targets BMW and Mercedes with premium SUVs in Middle East, Asia-Pacific
data.description:
Li Auto, one of Tesla’s major rivals in mainland China, has set its sights on the Middle East and Asia-Pacific, with its premium intelligent models poised to take on petrol car brands such as BMW and Mercedes-Benz.
The Beijing-based electric vehicle (EV) maker, which is actively building a sales network abroad, expected overseas deliveries to account for 30 per cent of its total by 2030, according to president Ma Donghui.
“It is inevitable that we will compete against BMW, Benz and Audi, which are viewed as luxury marques worldwide,” he told the South China Morning Post in an interview on Saturday. “Our vehicles have outperformed theirs in terms of performance and user experience, but it takes time to establish brand awareness because overseas consumers haven’t experienced our cars and haven’t yet been convinced of our superiority.”
Ma’s remarks came after Li Auto, known for its large SUVs that use extended-range battery technology, announced plans to expand into Asia-Pacific markets in May, forming partnerships with distributors in Cambodia, Laos and Macau.

On Saturday, the company signed distribution agreements with Al Fahim Motors in the United Arab Emirates and with Mohamed Yousuf Naghi Motors in Saudi Arabia as it seeks to make inroads in the Middle East.
Li Auto’s Li series SUVs, which have been well received on the mainland, have lured thousands of wealthy clients away from BMW, Mercedes and Audi.
These SUVs are equipped with small internal-combustion engines that generate additional power to charge the electric battery when needed.
The EV maker, founded in 2015, took the lead in transforming next-generation cars into “mobile homes”, where fittings such as refrigerators and sofa-like seats have enhanced passenger comfort and added another dimension to luxury car features.
Among premium Chinese EV makers, Li Auto was the first to turn a profit in 2024, buoyed by the sales of its Li series SUVs priced above 300,000 yuan (US$43,833).
Ma said the carmaker was currently executing its globalisation strategy, stressing that a complete sales and after-sales network would take at least three years to establish.
Initially, the company would focus on exporting Chinese-made vehicles, he said, adding that an overseas production base would be considered only when sales outside China reached a large volume.
At the ongoing Auto China in Beijing, the world’s largest car show, nearly all major Chinese EV assemblers have either unveiled their latest models designed for overseas markets or have announced growth targets for overseas markets, where their cars can command higher prices.
“We believe that Li Auto’s advantage today lies in our definition and interpretation of a car that can be developed into a comfortable living space,” said Wu Zuomin, Li Auto’s head of international business. “With this strength, we will first explore potential demand in overseas markets.”
On the mainland, carmakers’ average net margin per vehicle – the gap between the selling price and production cost – stood at about 5,000 yuan, according to Nick Lai, head of auto research for Asia-Pacific at JPMorgan Chase.
In October, he said that margin could rise fourfold to 20,000 yuan in overseas markets, where Chinese cars were sold at higher prices.
Leading Chinese EV builders from BYD to Leapmotor are all targeting a substantial increase in exports this year as they chase higher profits amid a slowing domestic market.
BYD, the world’s largest EV assembler, is targeting a 24 per cent increase in overseas deliveries this year, aiming to ship 1.3 million vehicles.
Xpeng, a premium EV maker, expects to deliver more than 90,000 vehicles abroad this year, or about 15 per cent of its targeted annual output.
Stellantis-backed Leapmotor aims to achieve overseas sales accounting for 10 per cent of its total deliveries in 2026, up from 5 per cent in 2025.

data.title: Hong Kong developers clear stock amid strong demand and shrinking supply
data.description:
Hong Kong developers released nearly 300 new flats to the market amid enthusiastic demand from potential homebuyers as the latest official data show completion of private residential units is set to decline this year and next.
By 6pm, all 218 units at the One Victoria Cove Phase I in Hung Hom, jointly developed by Henderson Land Development, Hysan Development and Empire Group, were sold, while at the Pavilia Farm III atop Tai Wai station in Sha Tin by New World Development (NWD) and MTR Corporation, all 75 flats had found buyers, according to agents.
The brisk sales came amid an estimated 8 per cent decline in the completion of new private residential units this year compared to the previous year, according to the latest data from the Rating and Valuation Department (RVD).
“Supply contraction is becoming the main theme for the next two years,” said Derek Chan Hoi-chiu, head of research at Ricacorp Properties. “This three-year trend of declining supply, coupled with relatively low interest rates, a stable financial market, and sustained purchasing power, is expected to support a 15 per cent increase in property prices for the year.”
There were 18,448 private homes completed by developers in 2025, down by 24 per cent from the previous year, according to the RVD. This will further decline to an estimated 16,975 units this year and 15,362 in 2027.
Hong Kong’s residential sales hit a four-year high in 2025 with 62,832 units changing hands, according to the Land Registry. Along with the higher sales, prices of second-hand units rose by 3.63 per cent, according to the RVD.
With improved sales and rising prices, the city’s residential property market officially reversed a three-year slump.
The downturn was mainly triggered by a tightening monetary stance after the Hong Kong Monetary Authority increased base rates on 11 occasions between March 2022 and July 2023, taking them to the highest level in nearly two decades, in lockstep with the US Federal Reserve.
A supply glut of new private residential units, which reached a record high of 24,300 in 2024, also weighed on the market.

The declining supply is forecast to give developers time to dispose of new completed units, allowing them to reduce discounts extended to buyers to dispose of unsold stock, which could in turn, boost prices in the resale market.
At the One Victoria Cove Phase I project, buyers could choose a flat with layouts of two to three bedrooms and sizes ranging from 339 sq ft to 642 sq ft. Discounted prices started from HK$6.38 million (US$814,000) and a discounted price per square foot of HK$17,365.
More than 7,600 applications were received on Friday night for the batch of units for sale, representing an oversubscription of more than 33 times, the developers said.
One Victoria Cove is the first large-scale inland-to-waterfront major network project in the Urban Renewal Authority’s Victoria Cove Area in Hung Hom and is close to the MTR’s To Kwa Wan station.
Meanwhile, at the Pavilia Farm III, flats made available to buyers comprised units with layouts of one to two bedrooms, ranging in sizes of 307 sq ft to 534 sq ft. Discounted selling price starts at HK$7.89 million and discounted selling price per square foot at HK$21,928.
After closing the registration period on Friday evening, the latest sale received more than 8,200 applications, representing an oversubscription of over 108 times, NWD said.
“Primary market transactions have reached nearly 1,600 units so far in April and the launches of multiple new projects have received strong demand from buyers,” said Louis Chan, vice-chairman and CEO for residential, Asia-Pacific, at Centaline Property Agency. “The total first-hand sales for the month could surpass 2,200 units.”

data.title: Doctor in long-delayed hearing over alleged blunder ‘thought baby was choking’
data.description:
A Hong Kong doctor accused of a blunder that left a boy permanently disabled 16 years ago said he did not return to the hospital to examine the infant immediately as he initially diagnosed the emergency as a choking case, the Medical Council heard on Sunday.
Testifying for the first time at the hearing, Dr Sit Sou-chi contested an allegation that he had failed to conduct all necessary and immediate investigations of newborn Li Yuanjian, the son of a mainland Chinese couple, after the infant had a seizure on December 22, 2009, at Baptist Hospital.
The child was left with cerebral palsy and quadriplegia, unable to care for himself for life.
Sit said he was first notified of the child’s condition at 4.30am by nurse Ho Kit-ha, who called him while he was sleeping.
He said Ho told him the baby had slightly choked on milk and his limbs had stiffened about an hour ago. But the boy was stabilised in 15 seconds after the nurse suctioned milk from his airway, the doctor said. The infant then remained stable, he added.
Sit claimed the nurse also said the baby was “OK and no problem” before 3.30am.
“My working diagnosis was a choking attack with all the clinical picture and symptoms I was presented, that’s why I wanted to observe. If it happened for the second time, it would be strange because feeding had stopped, and I would be thinking the diagnosis of neonatal seizure,” he said.
“I thought of [seizure], but without a recurrence, I cannot confirm that. It’s one of the possibilities, which is unlikely … a choking attack is more common.”

Sit said that, from his understanding, neonatal seizure could be caused by brain infection or electrolyte disturbances, among others.
He admitted that he never shared this choking diagnosis with the parents, but he insisted he told the nurse during the phone call.
Sit did not return to the hospital immediately, although he lived a 15-minute ride away, and ordered the baby to be put inside an incubator and have his blood oxygen saturation levels monitored.
The boy’s vital signs were also to be observed hourly, and Sit took the nurse’s suggestion of “nil by mouth”, or no food, water or medicine for the patient.
Sit asked the nurse to tell him immediately if the symptoms emerged again, and he would arrange further investigations.
He stressed that the instructions were related to seizure management, because an incubator would allow the nurse to monitor any abnormal movements and changes of colour in the infant more easily.
Despite two more convulsions between 6am and 7am, Sit was not notified again until 7.30am. He arrived at the hospital 15 minutes later, after which the child was referred to a public hospital.
Senior counsel Mike Lui Sai-kit, the legal officer of the case, voiced objection as the conversation in Chinese that Sit recalled was not in the witness statement and was never put to Ho, who testified on Friday.
Lui called it “fundamentally unfair and an abuse of the process”, and questioned the authenticity of Sit’s account of the conversation, which had never been presented before.
He said the nurse never told him the baby had a convulsion, but a meeting recording played at the hearing revealed that he told the parents on December 30 that “at 4.30am, I was told there was a suspected convulsion, but I didn’t know for sure”.
Sit explained that he used the word convulsion only because the child had already been diagnosed with meningitis, and he was not aware of convulsions beforehand.
Council chairwoman Grace Tang Wai-king questioned why Sit could not realise the severity of the situation when a nurse with experience in handling choking incidents called him at 4.30am.
“Other than choking, she was concerned because there were other symptoms and observations … but you didn’t seem to be concerned,” Tang said.
Tang also questioned why Sit left the child for the nurse to observe and to see whether the symptoms reoccurred, because if they did, it would be too late for the doctor to return to the hospital and examine the baby.

“If a nurse suspected, she would not have waited for an hour [to contact me] … it certainly gave me the impression that she did not think this was serious,” he said. “For the attacks [later], she chose not to inform me.”
Parents Li Zhijian and Peng Hongying also attended the hearing.
Li said he was surprised by the choking diagnosis, which he had not heard before.
“It is just a 15-minute trip from Sit’s home to the hospital. Why couldn’t he have visited the baby and checked on him to ensure it was not a seizure or meningitis?” Li said.
He said that while mistakes could happen, the person involved should admit them and take responsibility. Li said he refused to accept Sit’s account.
The hearing will resume on June 7.
The complaint was filed in 2010, but the case was delayed. A decision by the council to terminate the case last year sparked a public outcry and led to the resumption of the inquiry.

data.title: Can parents, children playing video games together level up their relationship?
data.description:
Parents and children who play video games together rate their relationships as 20 per cent stronger than those who do not, according to a survey by a Hong Kong non-profit organisation.
The Chinese YMCA of Hong Kong on Sunday urged parents to adopt a new approach to supervising their children’s gaming habits, encouraging families to take part in such activities together rather than just observing.
The group issued the call after conducting a survey of the experiences and attitudes of parents, young children and teenagers towards playing video games together.
“Instead of being an observer, or telling kids they should not play, parents should become participants and play with their children so they can know what they are playing and who they play with,” said Phoebe See Man-yan, the organisation’s coordinating secretary.
As part of its survey, the group polled 2,271 youngsters and teenagers aged six to 18 and 1,283 parents in their twenties, thirties, forties and older between August and November last year.
It also conducted in-depth interviews with 14 youngsters and eight parents in March this year to gather additional information.
The survey found that parents playing video games with their children was not a popular trend. Only 29 per cent of children and teenagers said they played with their parents, while 35 per cent of parents gamed with their children.
But the results showed that both children and parents who played video games with one another rated their levels of emotional connection, trust and communication about 20 percentage points higher than those who abstained.
Among the surveyed children and teenagers who played video games with their parents, 80.9 per cent agreed that they could work with their parents on a task – 20.6 percentage points higher than those who did not play with their parents.
A similar trend emerged among parents who gamed with their children, with 86.8 per cent agreeing that they could cooperate with their kids.
The figure was 21.9 percentage points higher than that of parents who did not take part in such activities with their children.

Another sign of a healthier parent-child relationship was that 73 per cent of children and teenagers in gamer families felt their parents understood their feelings.
In comparison, only 51.9 per cent of those in non-gamer families felt understood.
Trust levels also differed, with 81.6 per cent of children and teenagers who played video games with their parents saying they trusted their mothers and fathers. Only 63.5 per cent of those who had not could say the same.
While the parents polled agreed in general that video games were positive for their children, they were more reserved when it came to whether such activities could benefit their children’s personal growth and confidence.
On a six-point scoring system, with higher numbers indicating stronger agreement, parents scored an average of 4.31 when asked whether they felt playing video games was not a waste of their children’s time.
But when asked if they felt such games were important for their children’s growth, the average was 3.02.
“Even if half of parents think video games don’t bring growth, it is still of value, as parents agreed that playing video games allows for kids to relax and rest,” said Isaac Yeung Chi-hin, the Chinese YMCA of Hong Kong’s senior corporate development manager.
But Yeung said the younger cohort felt more strongly that video games could contribute to their personal growth, citing a long list of skills such as improved language learning from talking to other players, improved cooperation and problem-solving.
The survey also revealed the top games shared by parents and children.
The top spot went to the Super Mario series by Nintendo, an adventure game featuring the titular character Mario. Second was Roblox, an online game available on mobile and desktop platforms where players interact with one another as blocky, humanoid avatars.
Minecraft, a “sandbox” game where players can build tools, items and structures in virtual worlds, came third, while Nintendo’s Pokemon series, which features iconic characters such as Pikachu and is commonly played on Switch consoles, ranked fourth.
See also urged parents to refrain from double standards when it came to electronic device use and setting limits on video gaming for their children.
“We urge parents nowadays to move with the times. It’s not about telling kids what they can and cannot do any more, but walking alongside them and learning from them,” she said.
Wesley Wong, a 37-year-old father of three, said he played Roblox with his sons, aged six and nine, as well as sports and cooperative games on Nintendo’s Switch console.
“I can see the kids exercising patience, whether they can accept defeat and whether they will give up when the games get difficult,” he said.
Wong also expressed surprise at how fluent in English his sons were when they talked to other players online.

data.title: First section of Fanling Bypass to open soon. So how will Hongkongers benefit?
data.description:
The eastern section of Hong Kong’s new Fanling Bypass will be commissioned on May 3, marking the first major transport infrastructure project to be completed under the Northern Metropolis mega development.
The new bypass promises to cut travel times by 10 minutes and will serve as a vital roadway for the Fanling North New Development Area.
The South China Morning Post unpacks what the new bypass is and its significance.
1. What is the new bypass?
The Fanling Bypass (Eastern Section) is a new 4km (2.5 mile) carriageway that serves to link North district in the New Territories with urban areas. The dual two-lane carriageway encompasses at-grade roads, underpasses and viaducts.
The bypass starts at Fanling Highway near Kau Lung Hang in Tai Po and connects to Shek Wu San Tsuen and the Fanling North New Development Area. In between, it passes through the On Lok Tsuen Industrial Area and the Lunk Yeuk Tau Interchange.
Authorities said the bypass allowed motorists to travel between Fanling Lung Yeuk Tau, Luen Wo Hui and Fanling Highway without having to pass through the busy roads running through Sheung Shui’s and Fanling’s town centres.
The new route would help to shave off 10 minutes of travel time during peak hours, the government said.
Authorities said the bypass would also help divert traffic from the Fanling North New Development Area in the future.
The amount of traffic along existing major roads in Fanling and Sheung Shui, as well as the overall operational efficiency of the road network within North district, would also improve, the government said.


2. How does it help the Northern Metropolis?
The commissioning of the Fanling Bypass is considered to be the first major transport infrastructure project to be completed under the Northern Metropolis.
First announced in 2021, the Northern Metropolis megaproject aims to transform 30,000 hectares (74,132 acres) of land along the border with Shenzhen in mainland China into a dual engine for economic growth and housing.
According to the government, the bypass will support the development of the Northern Metropolis and the Fanling North New Development Area, which is situated within the wider megaproject.
The new development area comprises about 165 hectares and, once completed, will be able to house about 95,000 people and provide employment for around 14,400.
A 2024 Legislative Council paper estimated that construction of the full development area, which will also encompass Kwu Tong North, would cost about HK$32 billion (US$4.08 billion) overall.
Authorities said the new bypass would boost connectivity in nearby villages and create what they called “favourable conditions for urban-rural integration”.
The western section of the bypass is due to be completed at a later phase of the new development area’s construction.

3. What innovations were used in the bypass’s construction?
The government said the new bypass was built in a “complex construction environment” that spanned developed and rural areas, including existing road networks.
Two tenders were awarded for the project, each for 2km of roadway.
A section stretching between Shung Him Tong and Kau Lung Hang was awarded to a joint venture between the China Railway Construction Corporation (CRCC) and Paul Y Engineering for HK$3.34 billion, according to official records.
Meanwhile, the section between Shek Wu San Tsuen North and Lung Yeuk Tau was awarded to another joint venture of civil engineering firms, including Chun Wo Development Holdings and Kwan Lee Holding, for HK$3.5 billion.
In a first for Hong Kong, the CRCC joint venture deployed an award-winning “horizontal bridge rotation method” for bridge construction over the East Rail line.
The process involved building the bridge superstructure parallel to an obstacle and then rotating it into its final position overnight.
Authorities said the method significantly reduced both the time and extent of work conducted above live railway tracks and limited workers’ exposure to high-risk environments.
The government also highlighted that the construction of the cycle track and pedestrian bridge at the Lung Yeuk Tau Interchange used mainland-made, ultra-high-strength S960 steel, making it the first footbridge in the world to use the material.
It said the material, which is reportedly three times stronger than conventional steel, had helped to significantly reduce steel consumption and the weight of the bridge.

data.title: Tianqi Lithium favours China’s salt lakes over Africa amid resource nationalism
data.description:
Amid geopolitical tensions, China’s Tianqi Lithium has decided to skip Africa and will stick to domestic salt lakes to secure the supply of a battery mineral, according to its president Frank Ha Chun-shing.
The move comes as the oil shock stemming from Middle Eastern conflicts is driving up global demand for electric vehicles (EVs) and battery energy storage systems (BESSs).
Lithium, which can be extracted from hard rock and salt flat brines, can be processed into lithium carbonate, a raw material widely used in consumer end markets that include electronics and drones.
Tianqi, which operates the world’s largest lithium brines in Chile and the world’s largest hard-rock lithium mine project in Australia, is facing tighter restrictions in its overseas operations amid resource nationalism.
In Zimbabwe, the world’s seventh-largest supplier of hard-rock lithium, the government suspended the export of lithium concentrates in late February to force foreign miners to invest in local processing. Meanwhile, in Chile, Tianqi lost significant influence over the Salar de Atacama – the world’s largest lithium salt lake – after the state increased its control over the resource in January.

“Fractional policy oversight has emerged, making the global supply chain no longer a [single] piece,” Tianqi’s Ha told the South China Morning Post in mid-April.
Though Tianqi has no exposure to Africa, Ha said the firm still needed to invest in new lithium resource projects.
“But we will not go to Africa and instead eye the salt lakes like those in mainland China,” said the president of the Chengdu-based battery mineral supplier.
In response to the Zimbabwe export ban, two Chinese companies – Huayou Cobalt and Sinomine – have promised to build a lithium sulphate plant in the country, which is Africa’s top lithium supplier, accounting for 9.3 per cent of global mine production of lithium in 2024.
While China supplied more than 64 per cent of global lithium chemical products last year, only 16.5 per cent of worldwide lithium reserves are located in the country.
Tianqi acquired a 20 per cent stake in Shigatse Zabuye in Tibet in 2014, securing access to the Zabuye Salt Lake, the third-largest in the world and largest in Asia.
Earlier this month, Tianqi’s new chairwoman Jiang Anqi also noted that the firm would shift its focus to the exploration of domestic mines given current geopolitical risks.
“Against the backdrop of increasing uncertainty in the current external environment, we believe that the development of high-quality domestic lithium resources cannot be delayed any longer,” said Jiang, who in 2024 took over the helm of the company founded by her father, Jiang Weiping.
China’s lithium carbonate prices surged as high as 180,000 yuan (US$26,358) per tonne in January, tripling the level seen in June 2025, due to intensifying resource nationalism and rising demand for EVs and artificial intelligence data centres. The battery mineral was trading at 177,900 yuan per tonne on Friday.
Strong demand in the downstream market has contributed to outstanding financial performances by several lithium suppliers. Tianqi, for example, saw its first-quarter net profit skyrocket by as much as 1,818 per cent year on year to 1.7 billion yuan, after swinging to a net profit of 463 million yuan last year from a 7.9 billion yuan loss in 2024.
However, the lithium mineral market is getting crowded. Zijin Mining, China’s largest gold miner, ramped up its lithium carbonate output nearly 100 times to 25,500 tonnes last year, while China’s EV battery king Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd (CATL) plans to set up an investment arm to secure raw material supply.
Tianqi’s Ha has been advocating for a globally unified pricing system to protect producers of intermediate products from an inversion of pricing, as miners and battery makers are taking more profits from the industry.
With tightening national controls and profit squeezes, Ha would like to see fewer national restrictions on exports and a healthier industry profit distribution for a “win-win” situation.

data.title: Woman rescued after falling into sea while taking photos at a Hong Kong lighthouse
data.description:
A 46-year-old mainland Chinese woman was rescued on Sunday morning after slipping into the sea while taking photos at a lighthouse in Hong Kong’s Sai Kung.
Police received a report at 10.54am that a woman had fallen into the water at Fat Tong Mun, a sea channel between the southern tip of the Clear Water Bay Peninsula and the northern tip of Tung Lung Chau.
A source said the woman was taking photos with friends at Fat Tong Mun Lighthouse when she lost her footing and fell three metres (9.8 feet) into the sea.
Police said a couple passing by on a fishing boat managed to pull her from the water.
Marine police arrived at the scene and found the woman had regained consciousness. She was then taken to Siu Sai Wan Pier before being rushed to Pamela Youde Nethersole Eastern Hospital in Chai Wan for further treatment.
Known as Hong Kong’s “Eastern Gate”, Fat Tong Mun is a historic waterway near Clear Water Bay. It is home to the city’s oldest Tin Hau Temple, built in 1266, as well as ancient rock carvings. The site remains an important cultural landmark for seafaring traditions and heritage conservation.
The incident is the latest in a string of photo-taking accidents involving tourists in Sai Kung.
On New Year’s Day, a 21-year-old Guangzhou student fell 70 metres into the sea while taking photos near a cliff at Po Pin Chau, and later died in hospital.

data.title: Cinema Day attracted 201,000 film-goers. How does it stack up against last year?
data.description:
Hong Kong’s Cinema Day has drawn a record-breaking 201,000 film-goers, despite a marginal decline in occupancy rates compared with last year.
The Hong Kong Theatres Association, which organises the annual event, revealed the figures on Sunday, just a day after 52 cinemas offered HK$30 (US$3.83) movie tickets for 1,694 screenings.
“It marks a new high for the event, with 201,000 admissions and an average occupancy rate of 81 per cent,” a post on the promotion’s social media page read.
“Families, the elderly and young people joined. Cinemas were packed with a vibrant atmosphere!”
The footfall marked a 3.2 per cent increase from 2025’s event, when 194,755 people flocked to cinemas. But the overall occupancy rate fell by two percentage points from the 83 per cent recorded last year.
The association added that shopping centres, restaurants and retail outlets had launched promotions as part of this year’s event, boosting the city’s economy and cultural development.
Over the past three years, Cinema Day resulted in more than 600,000 admissions and generated over HK$44 million in box office revenue, the Culture, Sports and Tourism Bureau said.
The government’s Cultural and Creative Industries Development Agency subsidises the tickets through the Film Development Fund.
The government has said it hopes to cultivate the cinema-going culture among the public, while supporting the long-term development of the city’s film industry and boosting consumption.
The event was launched in 2023 after the Covid-19 pandemic and aims to revitalise the industry after three years of disruptions caused by business instability and mandated social distancing.

data.title: From supply chain to record growth: Shenzhen dominates China’s robotics sector
data.description:
Inside the busy aisles and booths of the Fair Plus robotics trade show in Shenzhen this week, the usual stunt spectacle was replaced by practical tasks: robots sorting supermarket stock, serving popcorn and transporting oversized boxes.
Among them, Shenzhen-based X Square Robot showed the real-world ability of its wheel-based humanoids, using the firm’s self-developed Wall-A embodied foundation model to pick up rubbish from the floor and place it in a bin.
Currently deployed in on-demand cleaning services in homes in Shenzhen, the Quanta X1 Pro robot on display handles chores like folding laundry and scooping cat litter.
By leveraging a sophisticated supply chain and a wide range of real-world applications, local firms such as X Square have helped Shenzhen become the country’s leading robotics powerhouse.

The total output of Shenzhen’s robotics industry reached a record in 2025, rising 20 per cent from a year earlier to over 242 billion yuan (US$35.4 billion), according to a whitepaper released at Fair Plus – short for Fair of AI and Robotics Plus – which ran from Wednesday to Friday.
Shenzhen accounted for 43 per cent of China’s total output of service robots, producing nearly 8 million units last year, while the city manufactured 194,900 industrial robots, accounting for a quarter of the national output, according to the whitepaper. That put Shenzhen at the top nationally for both core robotics categories, the paper added.
The revenue of Shenzhen’s robotics industry cluster jumped 34 per cent to 37.9 billion yuan last year. In terms of technological innovation, the city was home to 4,676 companies holding robotics-related patents in 2025, up nearly 20 per cent from a year earlier, according to the whitepaper.
Embodied intelligence – a branch of artificial intelligence that operates through a physical body – was identified for the first time by the Chinese government as a key future industry in a work report delivered at the annual legislative meeting in March.
Shenzhen has nurtured some of the country’s leading robotics start-ups, including Engine AI, which received praise from the international tech community for performing the world’s first front flip by a robot, and UBTech, the first Chinese humanoid robot maker to go public in Hong Kong.
The city has a world-leading hardware supply chain cluster that can effectively shorten the cycle for customised components, said Yang Qian, chief operating officer of X Square.
“Custom parts can be delivered in days, compared with months overseas,” Yang said. “The costs and time needed for product iteration in Shenzhen are only a tenth of those abroad, which can significantly reduce the financial and time burdens for mass production.”
Yang added that the local government was supporting the opening of industrial, retail and community scenarios for robot deployment, which would enable robotics firms to test products and iterate models quickly.
X Square was gearing up to expand its home robot line-up next month, with an upgraded humanoid powered by its next-gen Wall-B foundation model, Yang said.

data.title: Over 5,000 Guangdong motorists sign up for Hong Kong’s southbound travel scheme
data.description:
More than 5,000 Guangdong motorists have been approved to drive into Hong Kong’s urban areas under a southbound travel scheme as of March, while 90 per cent of the 3,500 travellers who booked to visit have stayed for one or two days.
The Transport Department said on Sunday that the urban access portion of the Southbound Travel for Guangdong Vehicles arrangement was operating smoothly and had become increasingly popular since its launch near the end of 2025.
The scheme currently grants access to Hong Kong for up to 100 vehicles a day from Guangzhou, Zhuhai, Zhongshan and Jiangmen.
It permits approved vehicles to enter Hong Kong during booked time slots and travel via the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge to stay in the city for a maximum of 72 hours.
The arrangement aims to integrate mainland Chinese private cars into the city’s road network to boost tourism, with local authorities highlighting the economic potential of the influx.
“Since its launch on December 23 last year and up until the end of March this year, the department has approved more than 5,000 applications and recorded over 3,500 travel bookings, with around 90 per cent of travellers staying in Hong Kong for one to two days,” the department said.
“Southbound travel visitors have higher spending power, bringing opportunities for Hong Kong’s tourism, retail and catering industries. This allows us to drive economic growth through the convenience of cross-border transport.”
Authorities have said they plan to expand the scheme to other cities in Guangdong province six months after its launch.
On November 15, the Airport Authority launched its “Park and Fly” service as a precursor to the southbound travel initiative. The arrangement allows travellers to simply “drop off their cars and catch their flights”.
The department also said on Sunday that more than 7,500 motorists had registered for the service to use the automated car park on an artificial island at the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge as of March, while about 3,000 vehicle bookings had been made.
“A ‘Park and Visit’ service designed to enhance local tourism experiences is currently under preparation for the next phase, which will further expand the seamless ‘self-drive and public transport’ park-and-ride experience,” it said.

data.title: Xpeng sets August goal to overtake Tesla’s self-driving tech in China
data.description:
Xpeng, the Chinese electric vehicle start-up often cast as a key rival to Tesla, has set an ambitious goal to surpass the US carmaker’s self-driving capabilities in China by August, according to co-founder, chairman and CEO He Xiaopeng.
The target highlights an intensifying technology race in the world’s largest car market, where manufacturers are increasingly leaning on advanced software to drive growth as government subsidies are phased out.
Although Tesla’s full version of its Full Self-Driving (FSD) system has yet to be approved for use in China, making direct comparisons difficult, He said Xpeng’s Vision Language Action (VLA) system already outperformed the US company in certain complex driving scenarios.
“We’ve set ourselves a goal: to fully outperform Tesla’s FSD in the Chinese market by August,” He said at a media briefing on Friday during Auto China 2026, the 10-day Beijing International Automotive Exhibition, which runs through May 3.
“China’s driving environment is more complex. If we can surpass it here, our overall capability should be stronger.”
As the only major foreign player in China’s fast-moving electric vehicle market, Tesla has become a benchmark – and a target – for domestic rivals competing on both technology and scale.
Chinese carmakers have increasingly shifted their focus towards innovation and in-house development after regulators urged the industry to move beyond a margin-eroding price war.
Xpeng, the third-bestselling Chinese EV start-up last year, said its latest VLA 2.0 system was designed to advance its vehicles from Level 2 (L2) driver assistance towards Level 4 (L4) autonomy. He has repeatedly argued in favour of skipping Level 3 (L3) altogether.

Elsewhere, Geely – China’s second-largest private carmaker after BYD – unveiled its self-developed robotaxi, the Eva Cab, at the Beijing car show, with mass production expected next year.
Demand in China’s EV market, meanwhile, has shown signs of strain.
Domestic sales fell 23.8 per cent year on year to about 2 million units in the first quarter, according to the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers, as buyers turned more cautious following a halving of the 10 per cent purchase tax exemption.
Xpeng’s aggressive timeline also comes as Tesla faces further delays in rolling out its most advanced assisted-driving features in China.
During an earnings call in the US on Wednesday, Tesla executives said FSD had yet to receive broader regulatory approval in China, pushing the expected timeline into the third quarter. That marks a setback from CEO Elon Musk’s earlier projection at a Texas shareholder event last November that approval could come by February or March this year.
Xpeng’s He said the company’s strategy of training its artificial intelligence systems on China’s dense and complex urban roads could give it an advantage in overseas markets such as Europe and Southeast Asia, where similar driving conditions are common.
To support that push, Xpeng plans to invest nearly US$2 billion in research and development this year, including work on flying cars. By next month, the company expects to enable autonomous driving without reliance on navigation maps.
The company is also pursuing a full-stack approach – spanning self-developed chips, world models and electronic architectures – aimed at strengthening long-term competitiveness.
Between the fourth quarter of this year and the second quarter of 2027, Xpeng plans to roll out VLA 2.0 test rides in several European markets, which accounted for more than half of its overseas sales last year.

data.title: Prognosis positive: how AI could help China bridge the medical resources gap
data.description:
Li Bin, a doctor at the First Hospital of Lanzhou University, was among the wave of Chinese consumers who snapped up Apple Mac Mini computers during the country’s frenzied adoption of the OpenClaw artificial intelligence agent earlier this year.
The young surgeon from Lanzhou, the capital of northwestern China’s Gansu province, bought one to run the open-source program, using it to develop an app to extract and organise information from doctor-patient conversations and lab report photos into structured medical records, eliminating the need for tedious and time-consuming manual data entry.
Thanks to AI, he said “a doctor with no coding training can build such applications at very low cost” to meet specific needs.
Li’s deft use of AI tools to streamline his medical work epitomises China’s national push to integrate AI into its healthcare industry, an initiative that experts say could help bridge gaps in the country’s medical resource distribution and raise efficiency in the sector.
As well as boosting the productivity of individual doctors, AI can also help improve medical service quality across a hospital, said Song Yuqin, deputy head of Beijing Cancer Hospital (BCH), at a recent healthcare industry forum.
For instance, she said, an AI system played a role in clinical trial research conducted at the hospital for lung cancer patients.
It ran automatically overnight, matching prospective participants against all lung cancer clinical trials to produce a ranked list of suitable studies for each, with notifications sent by 7am highlighting the top options.


Another AI-backed database at BCH could slash the time needed to prepare a research proposal from weeks to just one day, with doctors only required to input a few key details such as research priorities and sample data before a plan was generated, Song said.
“I think this matters to our patients as much as it matters to medical innovation,” she said, adding that BCH was spending tens of millions of yuan to upgrade its AI infrastructure this year.
The National Health Commission and four other agencies, including the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and the National Development and Reform Commission, rolled out a plan late last year to accelerate AI adoption across the healthcare sector.
Under the plan, China will push for AI-assisted diagnosis and treatment in its primary-level institutions – hospitals and clinics distributed across localities and mainly providing primary care – by 2030, a goal that would effectively make AI-enabled healthcare available nationwide.
The country will also encourage the use of AI to support medical imaging and clinical decision-making in hospitals at or above the middle rung of its three-tier system, while pushing broader integration across patient care, traditional Chinese medicine, public health, medical research and industry governance, betting on the technology to modernise the healthcare sector.
AI-powered primary care
Marrying AI and healthcare was also a hot topic at the “two sessions” – the annual meetings of the national legislature and the country’s top political advisory body in March – where about 100 healthcare representatives submitted 42 proposals on using AI to enhance the sector, ranging from drug research and smart hospitals to medical data governance and support for primary healthcare.
Geng Funeng, chairman of pharmaceutical firm Good Doctor Group, proposed funding the adoption of AI agents across primary healthcare institutions, a plan inspired by his close observation of how a community clinic doctor in Chongzhou, Sichuan province, used AI to help assess patients’ conditions and generate possible diagnoses and treatment options.
China had more than 1 million primary healthcare institutions by the end of last year, accounting for more than half of the country’s medical consultations. But many faced challenges stemming from outdated equipment, less experienced doctors and insufficient resources, Health News, a publication backed by the National Health Commission, reported this month.


The use of AI would help them bridge the medical resources gap with higher-tier hospitals, industry experts said.
Technical consultant Hu Sanduo said AI had improved healthcare quality and efficiency at the Beijing hospital where he worked.
It helped cut the time needed for the hospital’s doctors to write up medical records and clinical progress notes from around an hour to just a few minutes, in part by saving them time looking up and organising data, Hu said.
When monitoring and recording the condition of post-operative patients, overall fluid intake and output – including oral water intake, intravenous fluids and urine output – was an important indicator, but those records were previously scattered and manually entered by nurses at different times, making it difficult to track changes, he said. But the hospital’s adoption of AI allowed the system to automatically retrieve and aggregate the dispersed data, significantly improving efficiency for doctors.
Some cities, such as Suzhou in eastern China’s Jiangsu province, have embraced Beijing’s mandate by adding an AI-powered personal healthcare assistant to an app operated by their municipal health commissions.
Zhang Minglin, the head of Suzhou’s health commission, said the city had also deployed AI general practitioners that had provided more than 10 million consultations for residents.
A report released last year by European conglomerate Philips, which also makes medical equipment, said 89 per cent of Chinese patients believed AI could improve medical care, compared with a global average of 59 per cent.
It also said that 84 per cent of Chinese health workers believed AI could save more lives through early intervention.


Tech giants buy in
From Alibaba Group Holding to Baidu and Huawei, Chinese tech giants are capitalising on the national push and widespread faith in the technology as they race to offer services to consumers and doctors.
Alibaba’s fintech affiliate, Ant Group, operates the world’s largest AI healthcare app, A-Fu, with more than 1 billion users. People across the country, from big cities to remote regions, use the free app to interpret medical reports or seek advice on everyday health concerns. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
This month, Baidu launched what it said was the first OpenClaw-like AI agent for doctors in China. Dubbed Youyi Assistant, the agent aggregates credible medical sources, including more than 60 million research papers, to give doctors instantaneous assistance when making diagnoses.
Huawei has launched what it describes as an “AI operating system” for hospitals. When launching the platform this month, the company said it had integrated computing power, data and model resources for institutions and consolidated previously fragmented and stand-alone AI applications.
However, some Chinese medical experts have cautioned about the risks of using AI, especially when doctors rely on it too much.
Zhang Wenhong, a well-known infectious disease specialist, said that while AI could support healthcare by improving efficiency and assisting clinical workflows, it should not be seen as a substitute for doctors’ judgment.
He warned that over-reliance on AI tools in diagnosis and treatment could introduce risks if clinicians were too eager to defer to AI assessment without giving it a critical review.
Zhang said AI should remain a supportive tool under human supervision, with final medical decisions anchored in experienced physicians’ expertise and accountability.
Hu said that given the current trajectory of AI development, “hallucinations” that could lead to misdiagnosis were unlikely to be fully eliminated for a long time.
He said Zhang’s warning pointed to a more fundamental question: how should medical AI tools be designed to fit into a broader network of human collaboration, including their roles the allocation of responsibilities?
“Problems arising from development still need to be addressed through further development,” Hu said.

data.title: Hong Kong plans new trial of infecting male mosquitoes to halt offspring growth
data.description:
Hong Kong will trial a “mosquito-to-control-mosquito” strategy next year, under which the insect males are infected with bacteria to prevent their offspring from reaching maturity, following the city’s first local dengue fever case in over a year.
Announcing the strategy on Sunday, Director of Food and Environmental Hygiene Donald Ng Man-kit said the government would consider stepping up mosquito control measures with a lower threshold, as the city’s mosquito population rose more rapidly this month.
The first locally recorded dengue fever case since 2024 was reported last week, involving a 21-year-old man who was bitten by mosquitoes near a road construction site in Penny’s Bay on Lantau Island.
Authorities have carried out mosquito control and other preventive measures at the site and in the surrounding area.
Explaining the plan in a televised interview, Ng said that by infecting the males with a designated bacterium, any offspring produced after mating would fail to reach maturity.
He added that the strategy is expected to reduce the overall mosquito population.
Ng said scientific institutes in mainland China and Hong Kong had already begun preliminary work, with trials expected to start early next year.
Last year, the Environment and Ecology Bureau also pledged to explore a “mosquito-eat-mosquito” strategy used in Foshan to curb chikungunya fever. The method involves breeding elephant mosquitoes in laboratories and releasing them “proportionally” in waterlogged areas.
The insects prey on the larvae of the Asian tiger mosquito, a species that spreads chikungunya fever.
Ng noted that the Gravidtrap Index, which monitors the distribution and breeding of the Aedes albopictus mosquito, has already exceeded 20 per cent in several districts this month – a level not reached until May last year.

“Average temperatures this April were higher than last year, and rainfall also increased,” he said.
Under current policy, the department strengthens anti-mosquito work in a district if its Area Gravidtrap Index exceeds 10 per cent, including intensified mosquito control operations and public education.
Ng said that while the department had been persistent in its efforts, it would consider lowering the threshold that triggers intensified measures.
“We will assess the situation dynamically and consider lowering the threshold based on actual needs,” he said.
He added that the department would engage the industry next month and issue guidelines to help restaurants comply with regulations regarding a new policy allowing up to 1,000 venues to welcome dogs from June.
However, Ng stressed that the guidelines would serve as a reminder, and breaching them would not constitute a violation of licensing conditions.
He added that while restaurants were responsible for cleaning up dog excrement, the guidelines would state that they may charge necessary cleaning fees, provided customers are clearly informed in advance.
Restaurants will also be barred from allowing dogs to use customer utensils. Ng said authorities would consider whether the restaurant had issued a warning if pet owners fed dogs with such utensils before taking enforcement action.
With the new law coming into effect on May 8, the first batch of restaurants is expected to receive licences by June, with about 500 to 1,000 eateries included in the first phase.
Ng said no overall cap had been set for pet-friendly restaurants, adding that the quota would be reviewed based on the scheme’s implementation and the catering sector’s response.

data.title: Hong Kong boosts e-cigarette ban awareness in mainland China before ‘golden week’
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have stepped up publicity with mainland Chinese media ahead of a ban on possessing alternative smoking products, including e-cigarettes in public places, which will take effect before the Labour Day “golden week” holiday.
Director of Health Ronald Lam Man-kin said on Sunday that the Tobacco and Alcohol Control Office had conducted more than 11,000 inspections and issued about 2,200 HK$3,000 (US$383) fixed penalty notices so far this year. Of these, 23 were related to the expanded statutory no-smoking areas introduced in January.
Since January 1, no-smoking areas have been extended to within three metres (9.8 feet) of entrances and exits to childcare centres, residential care homes, schools, hospitals and designated clinics or health centres. Smoking is also banned at designated public transport boarding points when people are queuing in groups of two or more.
“The figure is not that high under our frequent inspection. It shows that the public generally understands, accepts and supports the measures. We have stepped up law enforcement, and it has yielded results,” Lam told a radio show.
From April 30, authorities will also ban the possession of alternative smoking products in public places, ahead of the holiday from May 1 to 5.
Lam said publicity at airports and land checkpoints had been stepped up, with officers distributing leaflets to visitors. He added that authorities had also worked with mainland media and Hong Kong’s travel sector to promote the new rules.
“It appears that tourists have a strong demand for Hong Kong’s hotels during the golden week holiday. We do not think the new rule will have a particular impact on travellers,” he said.

Secretary for Culture, Sports and Tourism Rosanna Law Shuk-pui earlier said hotel occupancy rates were expected to exceed 90 per cent during the period, with the government estimating about 980,000 mainland visitors — up 7 per cent from the same holiday last year.
Lam said the government hoped the new measures would further reduce the city’s smoking rate, which stood at 8.5 per cent last year.
“Following the new measure of banning the possession of alternative smoking products in public places, we believe their effectiveness will surface in the next stage,” he said.
“We hope to further lower the smoking rate to below 8.5 per cent and strive to become the city with the lowest smoking rate in the world.”
From April 30, anyone found carrying small quantities of alternative tobacco products — defined as no more than five e-cigarette pods, 5ml (0.7oz) of vape liquid, 100 heat sticks or 100 herbal cigarette sticks — in public places for non-commercial purposes will be issued a HK$3,000 fixed penalty notice.
Those who fail to produce valid identification or obstruct enforcement officers may face a summary offence carrying a maximum fine of HK$10,000.
Individuals found with larger quantities will be prosecuted instead of receiving a fixed penalty. A summary conviction carries a maximum fine of HK$50,000 and up to six months’ imprisonment.
Secretary for Health Lo Chung-mau earlier defended the 8.5 per cent smoking rate — defined as the proportion of daily cigarette smokers aged 15 and above — despite it exceeding the 7.8 per cent target.
He said the city’s tobacco control strategy had been effective, noting a 30 per cent decline in overall cigarette consumption.

data.title: Underwhelming or underrated? DeepSeek V4 shows “impressive” gains
data.description:
DeepSeek’s long-awaited flagship V4 model has fallen short of its domestic and US rivals, according to a new analysis, as the Chinese artificial intelligence firm struggles to replicate the market-shaking success of its earlier R1 release.
The company’s most advanced system, V4 Pro, ranked second among the world’s leading open-source models, behind Beijing-based Moonshot AI’s Kimi K2.6, benchmark firm Artificial Analysis said in a report on Friday.
While V4 Pro marked a clear improvement on its predecessor, V3.2, it still lagged top competitors.
V4 Pro scored 52 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, compared with 54 for Kimi K2.6, released earlier this week. By contrast, leading closed-source models from the US – OpenAI’s GPT-5.5, Anthropic’s Claude Opus and Google’s Gemini 3.1 Pro – scored 60, 57 and 57, respectively.
The results highlight the challenges facing DeepSeek as China races to narrow the AI gap with the US, amid intensifying competition at home and abroad as well as ongoing constraints on computing power.
Still, analysts noted that V4 delivered meaningful technical progress.
DeepSeek’s V4 was “impressive” for coming close to state-of-the-art performance, featuring an efficient one-million-token context window and the ability to run on Huawei Technologies’ Ascend 950PR AI chips, said Kyle Chan, a research fellow at the Brookings Institution, in a post on X on Friday.
A context window refers to the amount of information an AI model can process in a single pass. DeepSeek’s previous flagship model had a context window of 128,000 tokens.
In a report on Saturday, research firm SemiAnalysis said DeepSeek’s 90 per cent reduction in KV cache in a one-million-token context setting was “far more impactful than Google’s TurboQuant paper last month”.
The model is also notable for its compatibility with domestic hardware. Shortly after V4’s release on Friday, Huawei Technologies said its Ascend chip range and supernode systems would provide “full support” for running the model in inference.
But questions remain over how the model was trained. Brookings Institution fellow Kyle Chan said it was “equally notable” that DeepSeek made no mention of using Chinese chips during training, even as the model continued to trail US frontier systems.

That view was echoed by Council on Foreign Relations senior fellow Chris McGuire who said the release did little to shift the broader picture of US leadership in AI. He estimated the US remained about seven months ahead, adding that the absence of detail on training costs or hardware suggested V4 may have relied on restricted Nvidia Blackwell chips.
DeepSeek did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Market reaction was also more muted than during the debut of its earlier R1 reasoning model. While shares of Chinese chipmakers rallied on Friday following news of V4 and its integration with Huawei hardware, the release failed to trigger the kind of global shock seen last year.
When R1 launched, it wiped hundreds of billions of dollars from US equity markets, with Nvidia shares plunging 17 per cent in a single day. On Friday, however, Nvidia stock rose 4.32 per cent.
Artificial Analysis also flagged potential drawbacks in the new model. Despite gains in knowledge benchmarks, V4 Pro and its lighter V4 Flash variant recorded hallucination rates of 94 per cent and 96 per cent, respectively.
The firm further noted that V4 Pro is now more expensive than rival open-source models, including Kimi K2.6 and Zhipu AI’s GLM-5.1, as well as DeepSeek’s own V3.2. Even so, it remained significantly cheaper than leading closed-source systems, according to the benchmark firm.
SemiAnalysis called DeepSeek’s V4 “an exceptional engineering release” that was “just behind” the frontier. While its capabilities were not at the leading edge, it said the model could serve as a low-cost alternative to US closed-source systems.

data.title: Frenzy for Hong Kong luxury homes seen continuing after 156% sales rise
data.description:
Hong Kong’s sales of luxury homes more than doubled in the first quarter from a year earlier, helped by a buoyant stock market and attractively priced assets that drew the attention of wealthy investors including mainland Chinese buyers, according to analysts.
The segment was likely to record another increase in the second quarter, they added.
“With the wealth effect from accumulated gains in the stock market over the past two years, both local and mainland Chinese capital have seized the opportunity to redeploy assets from stocks to properties, shifting a portion of their funds into the real estate market,” said Derek Chan Hoi-chiu, head of research at Ricacorp Properties.
In the first three months of the year, 64 homes worth more than HK$100 million (US$12.8 million) were sold in the city, 156 per cent higher than the 25 transactions a year earlier, according to data compiled by CBRE. The total value of these transactions also more than doubled, to about HK$11.7 billion from HK$5.43 billion in the first quarter of 2025, the consultancy said.
Savills, meanwhile, tracked 58 luxury-home transactions in the period, with 18 of the assets acquired by verified mainland buyers. Another 33 buyers were of unknown nationality, and because some of these were mainland buyers, the consultancy estimated that mainland residents accounted for more than half of the upscale home deals.

Affluent Hongkongers also splurged on luxury properties. Lisa Kan Chin Chin, identified as the manager of Hong Kong singer and actor Eason Chan, bought a 3,627 sq ft flat at The Corniche in Ap Lei Chau for about HK$141 million on March 27, according to official records.
In addition, lingering uncertainties over the US-Israel war on Iran were burnishing Hong Kong’s appeal as a safe haven for global capital, which was likely to further boost investment in the city’s luxury properties, Ricacorp’s Chan said.
“It is anticipated that transactions for high-end residential properties valued at HK$100 million or more will remain steady with a slight upward trend in the second quarter,” he said, estimating that about 90 new and second-hand luxury homes would change hands in the June quarter.
As of Wednesday, meanwhile, the segment had seen 12 new and lived-in luxury home sales worth about HK$3 billion so far this month, according to data compiled by Ricacorp Properties.
The demand is supported by the resilience of the Hang Seng Index, according to CBRE. As of Friday’s market close, the benchmark index was unchanged this year despite the US-Israel war on Iran rattling global markets.
Additionally, Hong Kong retained the crown as the world’s most active market for initial public offerings, with a total of 37 companies raising about US$13.26 billion on the Hong Kong stock exchange’s main board in the three months ended March 31, according to data released by LSEG Data and Analytics. That represents a 453 per cent increase from the same period in 2025.
Luxury home prices in Hong Kong fell 22 per cent between 2021 and 2025, while they rose between 1 and 60 per cent in other major global cities such as London, New York, Singapore and Tokyo, according to Savills.
“More trophy-grade deals are resurfacing, signalling revived confidence and a greater willingness to transact at current price levels,” said Eddie Kwok, executive director for valuation and advisory services at CBRE Hong Kong. “With both prices and transaction volumes stabilising, the market appears to be bottoming out, supported by improving liquidity and renewed top-end buyer appetite.”
Among the more notable deals registered this year, a 5,577 sq ft luxury flat at The Legacy in Mid-Levels West went for HK$422.9 million and a 4,101 sq ft house at Repulse Bay Road sold for HK$372.9 million, both in January. In March, a 3,173 sq ft penthouse at the Ultima in Ho Man Tin set a record for price per square foot in the project, at HK$68,029, according to CBRE.
Midland Realty recorded a HK$269 million deal for a 4,710 sq ft home at The Legacy earlier this month, and a 3,601 sq ft house at Shouson Hill Road East was acquired for HK$180 million in February via a company share transfer.
A 2.25 per cent stamp duty hike for luxury properties worth more than HK$100 million, announced in February, had limited impact, CBRE’s Kwok said.
“For the second-hand market, more company transfers are expected, while for the primary market, developers will offer more discounts or rebates to offset the increment,” Kwok said. “In fact, market sentiment, stock market performance and interest rate movement expectations are more important in the luxury market.”

data.title: Chinese EV makers target global expansion at Auto China amid energy shockMost carmakers taking part in the exhibition have either debuted models designed for export markets or announced expansion plan
data.description:
Chinese electric vehicle (EV) makers at the Beijing auto show are shifting their focus to overseas markets, banking on opportunities from the Middle East crisis to boost profitability amid weak sales at home.
“Going global has become a top choice for Chinese EV builders as they struggle to break even,” said Phate Zhang, founder of Shanghai-based data provider CnEVPost. “The Middle East crisis is acting as a strong catalyst to bolster their overseas deliveries.”
Nearly all major EV assemblers taking part in Auto China, the world’s largest car show, have either unveiled their latest models designed for overseas markets, or have announced growth targets for markets in Europe and Latin America, where their cars can command higher prices.
State-owned GAC Group said it would enter overseas markets with three models, including a hybrid SUV with a range of 1,240km.
“The global automotive industry is currently facing historic opportunities from technological convergence and energy transformation,” the company said in a statement on Friday. “For GAC, internationalisation is a critical leap that will determine its future.”

The Beijing car show, which runs from Friday to May 3, is being held simultaneously in two venues – the China International Exhibition Centre and the Capital International Exhibition Centre.
A total of 1,451 models, of which 181 made their global debuts, were displayed at the show, according to the organisers, who added that the exhibition area of 380,000 square metres (4.1 million sq ft) was nearly 50 per cent larger than that of the previous edition in 2024.
With no clear end to the US-Israel war with Iran – and the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of oil and natural gas is transported, still closed – the popularity of battery-powered cars is rising in markets like Europe.
With the price of Brent crude rising above the psychologically significant US$100 per barrel mark on Friday, buyers were shying away from conventional cars, according to dealers and analysts.
Guangzhou-based Xpeng said it aimed to accelerate its overseas expansion this year, with Europe as its focus. The region accounted for more than half of its exports last year.
This year, Xpeng was looking to double its sales in Europe, president Brian Gu said in a media briefing at the car show.
On the mainland, carmakers’ average net margin per vehicle – the gap between the selling price and production cost – stood at about 5,000 yuan (US$732), according to Nick Lai, head of auto research for Asia-Pacific at JPMorgan Chase.
In October, he said that margin could rise fourfold to 20,000 yuan in overseas markets, where Chinese cars were sold at higher prices.
“We will begin exporting highly competitive models that are developed and produced here [in China],” said Nissan CEO Ivan Espinosa. “This is a natural next step as we serve customers in many destinations around the world, where expectations are evolving more dramatically than ever before.”
A Morgan Stanley report released early this week said that Chinese carmakers would export an average of 1.2 million units a year from 2026 to 2028, up 17 per cent from its previous forecast.
Global consultancy AlixPartners found that Chinese carmakers and suppliers aimed to make international markets their main profit driver by almost tripling overseas production by 2030, according to its new report published on Tuesday.
In November, McKinsey & Co predicted that up to five Chinese vehicle assemblers, leveraging their technology and production advantages in building electric cars, were likely to make the list of the world’s top 10 carmakers by 2030, disrupting the pecking order in the global automotive industry.
BYD, China’s EV champion, and Zhejiang-based Geely Holding Group, which owns Volvo Cars, ranked among the world’s 10 largest automotive groups in terms of sales last year.
